dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 for breathing problems at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, my name is Kevin, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having trouble breathing since about 6 o'clock this evening.
Can you tell me your name, where you are, and what day it is?
My name is Jeffrey. I'm at home in [CITY].
That's good. Do you know the exact time?
It's Saturday, and I believe it's around 10 o'clock.
Are you experiencing any pain or discomfort from the breathing issues?
No, just shortness of breath. It doesn't come with pain.
Do you have a history of COPD?
Yes, I've had COPD for several years.
Can you describe what the sensation of breathing feels like?
It feels like I get less air with each breath.
Can you rate how hard it is to breathe on a scale from 0 to 10?
It's about a 6 or 7. It started in the evening, around 6 o'clock.
Mr. Jeffrey, I am going to take your blood pressure and other vitals now.
Your blood pressure is 170 over 118.
Your pulse is 97 and regular.
Your breathing is 24 per minute and slightly labored.
Your oxygen saturation is 93% in room air. We don't see signs of pallor or diaphoresis.
Mr. Jeffrey, are you alert and aware of what's happening around you?
He is alert and oriented x4. Glasgow Coma Scale is 15.
Does your breathing worsen during physical activity?
Yes, especially outside in the heat.
His skin shows no excessive sweating. He doesn't appear in pain.
Are you having any pain besides shortness of breath?
Just the difficulty breathing, nothing else.
End-tidal CO2 is 26 in room conditions.
Can you look straight ahead? What day and time do you think it is?
It's about 9:30 or 10 o'clock, Saturday.
We'll be using a nebulizer to help your breathing.
Albuterol and Atrovent via nebulizer have started now.
O2 saturation improved to 99% with the nebulizer.
We are going to establish an IV now with an 18 gauge needle.
Blood pressure is now 180 over 112.
Heart rate is currently 94 beats per minute.
Breathing is at 25, with some decrease in work of breathing.
May we place a heart monitor on you?
Performing 4 lead ECG monitoring now.
12 lead EKG completes and displays sinus rhythm, incomplete left bundle branch block, and signs of LVH with possible lateral ischemia.
Mr. Jeffrey, do you have any medication allergies?
I don't have any known drug allergies.
The nebulizer treatment is complete. Do you feel any improvement?
A little bit, but I'm still short of breath.
Detected some expiratory wheezing during stretcher transfer, though it was not severe.
Partner, we will follow the respiratory distress protocols for this COPD exacerbation.
Where would you like to be transported to, Mr. Jeffrey?
I'd like to go to [COMPANY_NAME].
We are now assisting you to the stretcher. Continue breathing as comfortable as you can.
Rechecking vitals now. SpO2 is 99% after nebulizer treatment.
Pulse is 94, blood pressure is 180/112, and respirations are 25. He does not report pain.
Mr. Jeffrey, we are now moving you to the stretcher.
He is alert and oriented throughout the procedure-responding clearly to questions and commands.
Mr. Jeffrey was aware and oriented (A&O x4) throughout the whole scenario.
Per protocol, administered nebulizers and established IV access. 12 lead EKG showed signs of LVH and possible lateral ischemia.
The patient needs further hospital evaluation for respiratory function, as we've seen partial response to treatment.
Mr. Jeffrey was safely transferred to the ER. No transport complications noted.
His Glasgow Coma Scale remained at 15 (eye=4, verbal=5, motor=6). He was consistently alert and oriented.
EMT equipment including monitor leads and oxygen were safely removed after hospital transfer.
Upon arrival, vital signs were stable-pulse at 94, respirations 25 per minute, BP at 180/112, and SpO2 at 99%.
He reported no pain or discomfort during all stages of the transfer.
The patient refused sedation and handled all procedures with no issues.
All transport straps and positioners were applied properly before movement initiation.
The hospital was alerted in advance regarding his condition and the treatments carried out.
EKG strips and incident notes were handed over for ER reference.
SpO2 dropped slightly initially but improved after the nebulizer, remaining within safe limits.
The IV site was in the upper arm vein with no signs of reaction.
No adverse reactions to treatments or procedures were observed during the transport.
The patient indicated no significant relief from the nebulizer treatment during transport.
We're done for this call and ready for the next response.
